Roles & Independence Matrix
ESOP Transaction — Advisor Roles, Conflicts & Independence
Company
{{companyName}}
Date
{{generatedDate}}
§ 01Advisor Independence Requirements

Under ERISA, the ESOP trustee must be independent of the seller, the company, and all other transaction advisors. Independence ensures the trustee acts solely in the interest of the ESOP participants (employees) and not influenced by any party to the transaction.

Key principle: No advisor may have a financial interest that conflicts with their duty to the ESOP. The trustee's independence is the most critical — DOL scrutiny focuses on trustee independence first.
§ 02Roles & Independence Matrix

The following matrix identifies each advisor's role and independence status:

Advisor / Entity Firm Role Independent Conflict Status Notes
Forhemit Stewardship Forhemit SMC Transaction Advisor / Stewardship Yes Clear PBC — no equity interest in company
{{trusteeName}} {{trusteeFirm}} ESOP Trustee (Independent) Yes Clear Must be independent of seller and company
{{erisaCounselName}} {{erisaCounselFirm}} ERISA Counsel Yes Clear Represents trustee, not company or seller
{{qofeFirmName}} {{qofeFirmFirm}} QofE / Valuation Analyst Yes Clear Engaged by trustee, not company
{{sellerCounselName}} {{sellerCounselFirm}} Seller's Counsel N/A Clear Represents seller — not subject to ERISA independence
{{lenderName}} {{lenderFirm}} SBA Lender Yes Clear Arm's-length lender — no ownership interest

If any advisor is flagged as having a conflict, the entry should be updated to show the conflict status as Conflict and a note describing the nature of the conflict and any waiver or mitigation.

§ 03Independence Checklist

For each advisor claiming independence, the following should be verified:

1.No ownership interest. The advisor does not own stock, options, or any equity in the company or any related entity.
2.No compensation conflicts. The advisor's compensation is not contingent on the transaction closing or the appraised value.
3.No family relationships. The advisor does not have a family relationship with the seller, company management, or other advisors.
4.No prior business relationship. The advisor has no ongoing business relationship with the seller or company outside of this transaction (unless disclosed and determined to be immaterial).
5.Conflict check completed. A formal conflict check has been conducted and documented.
6.ERISA compliance. The advisor meets the independence requirements under ERISA §406 and DOL regulations.
§ 04Conflict Resolution Protocol

If a conflict of interest is identified at any point during the transaction:

1.Disclosure. The advisor must immediately disclose the conflict to all parties in writing.
2.Assessment. ERISA counsel will assess whether the conflict is waivable under ERISA and DOL regulations.
3.Remediation. If the conflict is material and not waivable, the advisor must be replaced. If waivable, the trustee must document the basis for the waiver.
4.Documentation. All conflicts, waivers, and remediation actions must be documented in the transaction file.
